elements.sent-2411-001: Human walkers traverse diverse environment and demonstrate different gait locomotion and energy cost on granular terrains compared to solid ground.
elements.sent-2411-002: We present a stiffness-based model predictive control approach of knee exoskeleton assistance on sand.
elements.sent-2411-003: The gait and locomotion comparison is first discussed for human walkers on sand and solid ground.
elements.sent-2411-004: A machine learning-based estimation scheme is then presented to predict the ground reaction forces (GRFs) for human walkers on different terrains in real time.
elements.sent-2411-005: Built on the estimated GRFs and human joint torques, a knee exoskeleton controller is designed to provide assistive torque through a model predictive stiffness control scheme.
elements.sent-2411-006: We conduct indoor and outdoor experiments to validate the modeling and control design and their performance.
elements.sent-2411-007: The experiments demonstrate the major muscle activation and metabolic reductions by respectively $15$\% and $3.7$\% under the assistive exoskeleton control of human walking on sand.
elements.sent-2411-008: Humans often traverse real-world environments with a variety of terrains that can disrupt steady gait and require additional effort~\cite{kowalsky2021human .}
elements.sent-2411-009: While walking on solid ground is well-studied, navigating granular terrains like sand poses significant challenges~\cite{dewolf2019off .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Studies have shown that walking on sand requires large ankle plantarflexion in stance phase and increased knee and hip flexion during leg swing~\cite{van2017effect .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Substantial changes on walking kinematics and greater postural instability were observed for walking on sand~\cite{pinnington2005kinematic .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Similar studies of walking on compliant substrates demonstrated elevated activity and great mechanical work through large excursion and maximum flexion in hip and knee joints~\cite{grant2022does .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Recent work in~\cite{Zhu2024SandJBME offered ground reaction forces and gait datasets with biomechanics of walking on solid ground and sand.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although walking on granular terrain (e.g., sand) has been explored, limited studies were reported for using wearable assistive devices such as exoskeletons for enhancing the walking stability and reducing metabolic expenditure.
elements.sent-2411-015: Recent advancements in wearable exoskeletons and prosthesis development have shown promising capability in restoring ambulatory function~\cite{gehlhar2023review , aiding postural adjustment, reducing muscle load~\cite{Zhu2021Design , and decreasing metabolic cost during locomotion~\cite{panizzolo2019metabolic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Various control methods have been developed, including quasi-stiffness approaches that match target profiles~\cite{huang2022modeling .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recent advancements include reinforcement learning for impedance control parameter configuration in knee prostheses~\cite{wu2022reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Novel impedance modulation strategies have been proposed for lower-limb exoskeletons to assist sit-to-stand movements while preserving wearer control~\cite{huo2021impedance , and for soft exoskeletons to aid push-off on various terrains~\cite{li2022human .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Trajectory tracking controls have been applied to lower limb exoskeletons to personalize and stabilize user gait movements with adaptive strategies~\cite{sharifi2022autonomous,shushtari2021online .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Model predictive control (MPC) has been developed for compliant device behavior~\cite{bednarczyk2020model and on-the-fly transitions between assistance modes~\cite{HunteAIM2020,jammeli2021assistive .. \setcounter{figure {1.}}}}

Heel strike (HS) and toe-off (TO) stand for heel-strike and toe-off events, respectively, and R and L for right and left legs, respectively.} \label{fig:biomechanics}
elements.sent-2411-021: All aforementioned exoskeleton applications were mainly for human walking on solid ground and the developed controllers cannot be directly applied to granular terrains.
elements.sent-2411-022: To address the knowledge gap, we focus on designing and evaluating a knee exoskeleton for walking assistance on sandy terrains, aiming to enhance ambulation ability while reducing metabolic cost.
elements.sent-2411-023: We propose a model predictive stiffness control design of human-exoskeletons to provide assistive knee joint torque and regulate walking gait on sand.
elements.sent-2411-024: A stiffness-based computation of desired knee torque is first designed using the observations of walking locomotion on sand and solid ground.
elements.sent-2411-025: A neural network is used to model human foot-terrain interactions and compute resultant ground reaction forces (GRFs) from measurements by wearable inertial measurement units (IMUs).
elements.sent-2411-026: Both desired knee torque and external GRFs are treated as inputs of the human-exoskeleton dynamic model.
elements.sent-2411-027: Finally, an MPC framework is formulated to regulate assistive torque throughout the gait phase.
elements.sent-2411-028: Both indoor terrain and outdoor experiments are conducted to validate the controller and demonstrate its efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-029: The main contribution of this work is twofold.
elements.sent-2411-030: To the authors' best knowledge, this is the first study to propose a stiffness-based MPC for knee exoskeletons assistive control that is specifically designed for walking on sand.
elements.sent-2411-031: Human walking dynamics on yielding terrains such as sand differ significantly from that on solid ground, and the proposed control strategy addresses these unique challenges.
elements.sent-2411-032: Second, the integration of a machine learning-based approach to estimate GRFs in real-time using wearable IMUs enables the exoskeleton controller to adapt to varying terrain conditions.
elements.sent-2411-033: This real-time adaptability enhances the system's ability to regulate gait, providing effective assistance in complex environments.

elements.sent-2411-042: Although the hip and ankle joints contribute to propulsion and mechanical work during walking on sand, the knee joint plays a key role in stabilizing and controlling gait, especially on granular terrain.

elements.sent-2411-049: We use the stiffness-based model to estimate knee joint torques in real time.
elements.sent-2411-050: Similar to~\cite{huang2022modeling , a Sigmoid function is considered to capture a smooth transition between stance and swing phases of the gait cycle, that is, \begin{equation}S(\bs{\theta}_k)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-a f(\bs{\theta}_k)}}, \label{eq0}\end{equation} where $\bs{\theta _k=[\theta_{kr \;\, \theta_{kl ]^T$, $\theta_{kl}$ and $\theta_{kr}$ are the left and right knee joint angles, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Function $f(\bs{\theta _k)=(\theta_{kr -\theta_{kl )-b$ represents an estimated optimal hyperplane that divides the gait cycle into two phases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The parameters $a$ and $b$ are optimized based on collected human subject data.
elements.sent-2411-053: Using~\eqref{eq0 , the estimated human knee moment is calculated by \begin{equation}\hat{\tau}_{h}=\left[1 - S(\bs{\theta}_k)\right] k_{st}(\theta_{ki}-\theta^0_{st})+S(\bs{\theta}_k) k_{sw}(\theta_{ki}-\theta^0_{sw}) \label{esttorq}\end{equation} with $i=l,r$ for left and right legs, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In~\eqref{esttorq , $k_{st}$ ($k_{sw}$) is the joint stiffness in the stance (swing) phase, and $\theta^0_{st}$ ($\theta^0_{sw}$) is the equilibrium angle for stance (swing) phase.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Model parameters such as $k_{st}$, $k_{sw}$, $k^0_{st}$, $k^0_{sw}$, $a$, and $b$ are estimated by minimizing the sum of squared errors between the estimated and actual knee moments.
elements.sent-2411-056: The actual knee torque is calculated from the ground-truth GRF measurements.
elements.sent-2411-057: We use a machine learning-based model to predict the GRFs.
elements.sent-2411-058: The model utilizes both temporal and spatial features from IMU measurements, offering a practical, non-invasive solution for real-time GRF estimation on sand by capturing motion and force data without direct ground contact.
elements.sent-2411-059: Fig.~\ref{fig:network illustrates a dual-pathway machine learning model for real-time GRF prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Input data are from IMUs mounted on the stance leg's shank, heel, and toe; see Fig.~\ref{fig:Model .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Similar to~\cite{YigitTASE2022 , only the accelerations in the sagittal plane ($x$- and $z$-axis) and the frontal-axis gyroscopic measurements ($y$-axis) from each IMU are used as the input for the machine learning model, i.e., $\bs{X _t=[a_x(t) \; a_z(t) \; \omega_y(t)]^T$ from all three IMUs at time $t$.. \setcounter{figure {2.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width = 3.35in]{./figures/Network.eps} \caption{Schematic of the machine learning-based dual-pathway GRFs prediction scheme.} \label{fig:network}
elements.sent-2411-062: The first pathway employs a bidirectional long short-term memory (Bi-LSTM) layer with 64 units to detect foot contact periods and gait phases.
elements.sent-2411-063: By processing data bidirectionally, the Bi-LSTM simultaneously learns from both past and future contexts.
elements.sent-2411-064: The hidden state output $\bs{h _t$ from the Bi-LSTM captures the temporal dynamics essential for identifying these periods and phases.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The second pathway utilizes a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to estimate terrain conditions and continuous GRF magnitudes during the detected stance phases.
elements.sent-2411-066: This dual-pathway architecture is designed to capture the relationships between temporal movement sequences (e.g., $ \ldots,\bs{X _{t-1 ,\bs{X _{t ,\bs{X _{t+1 ,\ldots$) and their spatial configurations for accurate GRF prediction.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: A sliding window length of 64 ms was used to enable timely updates for real-time application.
elements.sent-2411-068: Outputs from LSTM and MLP are combined via dot product, emphasizing temporal-spatial feature interaction for a more accurate GRF estimation.
elements.sent-2411-069: A final dense layer with linear activation predicts GRFs.
elements.sent-2411-070: The model is trained using mean squared error loss and Adam optimizer, with early stopping and learning rate scheduling for regularization and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-071: This architecture is hypothesized to outperform traditional single-pathway models~\cite{alcantara2022predicting by explicitly modeling both temporal progression and spatial configuration of movements.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Fig.~\ref{fig:control illustrates the overall design for the knee exoskeleton control.}

elements.sent-2411-089: The MPC is formulated as follows, \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\min_{\bs{\tau}_E} \sum_{i=m}^{m+H} [w_1(\Delta \dot{\bs{q}}(i))^2 + w_2 (\Delta \bs{\tau}_e(i))^2 + w_3 (\Delta \bs{\tau}_e^d(i))^2], \label{mpca} subj. to:~ \bs{q}(i+1)= \bs{q}(i)+\Delta t \dot{\bs{q}}(i), \label{mpcb} \dot{\bs{q}}(i+1)= \dot{\bs{q}}(i)+\Delta t\bs{M}^{-1}_i\Bigl[\bs{\tau}_e(i)+\hat{\bs{\tau}}_h(i)+ \nonumber \hat{\bs{\tau}}_{ext}(i)-\bs{C}_i\dot{\bs{q}}(i)-\bs{G}_i\Bigr], \label{mpcc} q \leq \|\bs{q}(i)\| \leq \bar{q},\; \underline{\dot{q}} \leq \|\dot{\bs{q}}(i)\| \leq \bar{\dot{q}}, \underline{\ddot{q}} \leq \|\ddot{\bs{q}}(i)\| \leq \bar{\ddot{q}},\; \,i=m,\cdots,m+H, \label{mpce}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $H \in \mathbb{N}$ is the prediction horizon, $m\in \mathbb{N}$, $\Delta t$ is the time step, and $q$ ($\bar{q}$), $\dot{q}$ ($\bar{\dot{q }$), and $\ddot{q}$ ($\bar{\ddot{q }$) are the minimal (maximal) bounds for the joint angle, velocity, and acceleration, respectively.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-094: While other control strategies might achieve joint angle trajectory tracking, MPCâ€™s predictive capabilities are essential for managing the non-linear walking dynamics on sand.
elements.sent-2411-095: By optimizing control inputs over a horizon, MPC demonstrates particularly effectively maintaining balance on deformable surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-096: The objective of the MPC has two key aspects: follow optimal joint angle trajectories and minimize the effort required by both the human and the exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-097: The cost function was chosen to ensure smooth torque assistance and stability, thus minimizing the physical load on the user.
elements.sent-2411-098: Eight able-bodied healthy subjects (6 males and 2 females, age: $24.8 \pm 2.7$, height: $168 \pm 7.8$~cm, weight: $65.5 \pm 9.5 $~kg) participated in experiments, with all eight joining the indoor trials and five of them also participating in the outdoor trials.
elements.sent-2411-099: All participants were self-reported to be in a good health condition.
elements.sent-2411-100: An informed consent form was signed by each subject and the experimental protocol was approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB) at Rutgers University.
elements.sent-2411-101: The top figure in Fig.~\ref{fig:devices shows the wearable devices and their interconnections in experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Six IMUs (from LP-RESEARCH Inc.) were mounted on predefined locations on left and right shanks, heels and toes.
elements.sent-2411-103: We carefully checked and calibrated the sensors before each trial to minimize the potential for sensor movement during walking.
elements.sent-2411-104: These IMUs and the encoders of the bilateral knee exoskeletons were the primary sensing sources for exoskeleton control~\cite{ZhuRAL2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-105: IMU data was wirelessly transmitted to a high-level micro-computer (NUC7i7DNK from Intel Corp.) for real-time estimation of human joint moments and GRFs.
elements.sent-2411-106: The MPC solver, implemented via customized Python scripts on the micro-computer, generated torque commands.
elements.sent-2411-107: These commands were relayed to a low-level microcontroller (from PJRC Teensy) for exoskeleton torque control via CAN bus.
elements.sent-2411-108: The control systems parameters were given as: $\theta_{st ^0 = 8.7$~deg, $\theta_{sw ^0 = 68.7$~deg, $k_{st = 0.047$, $k_{sw = 0.012$, $a = 0.19$, $b = 3.85$, $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 0.15$, $w_3 = 0.5$, $H = 50$, $\Delta t = 0.04$~s, and $\alpha = 0.3$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Fig.~\ref{fig:indoor shows the indoor experimental setup.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Ground-truth lower-limb kinematics were captured using an optical motion capture system (10 Vicon Bonita cameras from Vicon Ltd.) A three-segment walkway ($7.5$~m long, $0.76$~m wide, and $0.14$~m high) was constructed for indoor experiments.
elements.sent-2411-111: Segment $1$ was built with reinforced plywood with embedded force plate $\#1$ (model ACG-O from AMT Inc.) to replicate a solid ground condition.
elements.sent-2411-112: Segment $2$ was a sand-filled box with buried force plate \#2 (from Bertec Corp.) 14 cm beneath the surface.
elements.sent-2411-113: GRF measurements were calibrated for sand layer thickness~\cite{Zhu2024SandJBME .}
elements.sent-2411-114: Segment $3$, made of the same material as Segment $1$, ensured natural gait transition from sand.
elements.sent-2411-115: The three-segment walkway design ensures that the subjects employ the consistent walking strategy throughout the trial.
elements.sent-2411-116: For evaluation purposes, subjects were equipped with eight wireless EMG sensors (from Delsys Inc.) on the right leg, monitoring rectus femoris (RF), vastus lateralis (VL), vastus medialis (VM), biceps femoris (BF), semitendinosus (SEM), tibialis anterior (TA), lateral gastrocnemius (LG), and medial gastrocnemius (MG).
elements.sent-2411-117: The EMG data were sampled at $1500$~Hz, followed by notch filtering ($58$-$62$~Hz) to remove line noise and band-pass filtering ($30$-$500$~Hz with $4$th-order zero-phase Butter-worth filters).
elements.sent-2411-118: For indoor experiments, subjects participated in four groups of experiments, including Group~A: no exoskeleton; Group~B: unpowered exoskeletons; Group~C: exoskeletons with the baseline controller from~\cite{huang2022modeling ; and Group~D: exoskeletons with the proposed MPC stiffness controller.}
elements.sent-2411-119: The baseline controller was selected because it outperformed the most impedance controllers in minimizing muscle activation.
elements.sent-2411-120: The subjects were instructed to walk in their preferred manner in one direction starting at Segment $1$.
elements.sent-2411-121: For the first $5$ trials, the subject was required to walk on the walkway to get familiar with the sensing devices and the terrain conditions.
elements.sent-2411-122: After that, $5$ trials of each group were conducted, and paired sample t-tests were used for statistical comparisons across the groups, resulting in a total of 40 trials per group.
elements.sent-2411-123: Ten-minute breaks were provided between groups, and the sand surface was flattened before each trial.
elements.sent-2411-124: For analysis, the root-mean-square (RMS) and maximal values of each muscle's EMG signal were extracted for the steps in each group and terrain, averaged, and then normalized to the RMS or peak values in Group A.
elements.sent-2411-125: Outdoor experiments (see bottom figure in Fig.~\ref{fig:devices ) involved subjects wearing the knee exoskeleton and VO2 Master metabolic measurement device (from Vernon Inc.).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Participants were instructed to follow marked trajectories for approximately $10$ minutes ($500$ m) under the four groups (A-D), assigned randomly to eliminate order bias.
elements.sent-2411-127: Additionally, participants were instructed to maintain a consistent walking pace as their choices for comfort and capability.
elements.sent-2411-128: This approach ensured that the data collected would accurately reflect the impact of the exoskeleton under varied yet controlled conditions.
elements.sent-2411-129: Throughout the trials, the maximum rate of oxygen from the VO2 Master device was recorded for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-130: Key evaluation metrics included: (1) knee joint torque estimation and GRF prediction accuracy; (2) impact on lower-limb muscle activation; and (3) controller performance in reducing metabolic costs on different terrains.
elements.sent-2411-131: Results from indoor experiments were used to address the first two objectives, while outdoor experiments were conducted to evaluate metabolic costs over extended periods.
elements.sent-2411-132: To obtain accurate subject-specific dynamic models in~\eqref{eq:model , a system identification is required for each subject.}
elements.sent-2411-133: To do so, two experiment trials were conducted for each subject.

elements.sent-2411-217: Human walkers encounter diverse terrains, such as sand and solid ground, which result in varied gait locomotion and energy costs.
elements.sent-2411-218: This study aims to design and validate a stiffness-based model predictive control approach for knee exoskeleton assistance with walking on sand.
elements.sent-2411-219: A comparative analysis of gait and locomotion on sand versus solid ground is conducted.
elements.sent-2411-220: A machine learning-based estimation scheme is developed to predict ground reaction forces in real time.
elements.sent-2411-221: These predictions, combined with human joint torque estimates, are used to design a knee exoskeleton controller that employs a model predictive stiffness control strategy.
elements.sent-2411-222: The experiments demonstrate significant differences in lower limb kinematics and kinetics.
elements.sent-2411-223: Reductions in major muscle activation and metabolic cost during assisted walking on sand are also observed with $15\%$ and $3.7\%$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-224: The proposed knee exoskeleton control framework improves walking efficiency on granular terrains by reducing both muscular and metabolic demands.
elements.sent-2411-225: Human often traverse real-world environments with a variety of terrains that can disrupt steady gait and require additional effort~\cite{kowalsky2021human .}
elements.sent-2411-226: While significant research has focused on walking on solid ground~\cite{camargo2021comprehensive , real-world locomotion often occurs on granular terrains like sand, and this poses unique challenges due to the terrain's deformable and shifting properties~\cite{dewolf2019off .}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Granular terrains significantly alter the biomechanic characteristics of walking gait, including joint-level kinematics, kinetics, and energy expenditure.

elements.sent-2411-239: All aforementioned exoskeleton applications were primarily designed for human walking on solid ground, and the developed controllers cannot be directly applied to granular terrains.
elements.sent-2411-240: Moreover, the effect of knee exoskeleton control on joint moments among the ankle, knee, and hip joints on sand remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-241: To bridge this gap, we hypothesize that: (1) knee robotic assistance can reduce muscle activation and energy expenditure during walking on sand; and (2) knee exoskeleton control would alter the distribution of joint moments and joint angles among the ankle, knee, and hip joints for adapted walking efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-242: To test these hypotheses, we focus on designing and evaluating a knee exoskeleton for walking assistance on sandy terrains, aiming to enhance ambulation ability while reducing metabolic cost.
elements.sent-2411-243: Both indoor terrain and outdoor experiments are conducted to validate the controller performance.
elements.sent-2411-244: The contributions of this study are twofold: (1) a detailed analysis of joint-level biomechanics during walking on solid ground and sand, focusing on GRFs, joint torques, and energy cost; and (2) the design and validation of an adaptive exoskeleton control framework for granular terrains, emphasizing biomechanical insights and real-time adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-245: By addressing these biomechanical challenges, this study not only extends the applicability of wearable assistive devices to diverse environments but also provides novel insights into the dynamics of human-exoskeleton interaction on yielding terrains.
elements.sent-2411-246: The proposed stiffness-based MPC framework improves energy efficiency and gait stability on compliant surfaces, offering significant implications for assistive device design and rehabilitation.
elements.sent-2411-247: By examining the distribution of joint moments and angles across the hip, knee, and ankle joints, we highlight the role of knee assistance in redistributing mechanical effort and reducing metabolic cost.

elements.sent-2411-303: For the hip joint, two main regions of statistical significance ($p<0.001$) were observed within the gait cycle under both controller, i.e., during early to mid swing phase.
elements.sent-2411-304: Under the MPC controller, the knee joint exhibited two significant regions of statistical significance during the mid stance ($p<0.01$) and terminal swing phase ($p<0.05$) of the gait cycle.
elements.sent-2411-305: Regarding the ankle joint, no region of statistical significance throughout the gait is obtained under either controller.

elements.sent-2411-308: Although the exoskeleton applied the torques only at the knee joint, the joint torque profiles change significantly at the hip and ankle joints as well.
elements.sent-2411-309: The hip joint displayed an increased extension moment on both terrains with the exoskeleton during early swing phase, with walking on sand showing more significant increases and longer periods, corresponding to the larger hip flexion angles.
elements.sent-2411-310: On solid ground, the knee joint exhibited a characteristic biphasic pattern with peak extension moments during early stance and late stance.
elements.sent-2411-311: On sand, the MPC controller leads to higher knee joint moments during the late stance to early swing phase compared to both the unpowered exoskeleton (Group~B) and the baseline controller (Group~C).
elements.sent-2411-312: The knee moments during late stance and early swing phase was significantly increased across all conditions ($p<$ 0.001).
elements.sent-2411-313: In contrast, the ankle joint moments demonstrated increased variability on sand, particularly during push-off.
elements.sent-2411-314: Group~D showed a significant reduction ($p<$ 0.001) in peak ankle plantarflexion moments compared to other three groups, while the baseline controller increased the plantarflexion moments significantly ($p<$ 0.001).

elements.sent-2411-319: Walking on sand introduces significant biomechanical challenges, as reflected in the altered joint angle and moment profiles.
elements.sent-2411-320: Analysis of knee joint angles on sand (Fig.~\ref{fig:AngleComparison ) revealed that wearing the exoskeleton constrained the subject's knee joint movement to some degrees.}
elements.sent-2411-321: For instance, the unpowered and MPC-controlled modes made less flexion within the certain stance phase (i.e., $25$--$40$\% of gait phase), and for Groups~B, C and D, the maximum knee flexion of swing phase decreased compared with natural walking without exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-322: However, the MPC allowed for a gait more closely mimicking unassisted walking compared to other conditions.
elements.sent-2411-323: The observed differences in ankle plantarflexion and hip flexion angles further emphasize the role of multi-joint coordination in navigating granular terrains.
elements.sent-2411-324: Similarly, joint moment analysis revealed a redistribution of mechanical effort across the lower limbs, especially on sand.
elements.sent-2411-325: The knee joint exhibited significantly increased extension moments throughout the stance phase under the MPC controller, highlighting its compensatory role in maintaining stability and propulsion.
elements.sent-2411-326: This redistribution aligns with the control intent of reducing reliance on hip and ankle propulsion to compensate for the COM sinkage when walking on sand by augmenting knee joint contribution.
elements.sent-2411-327: Fig.~\ref{fig:angles shows a flatter knee flexion profile during the stance phase on sand, indicating the knee's role in maintaining stability.}
elements.sent-2411-328: Supporting knee flexion and extension reduces the need for compensatory strategies that would otherwise heighten ankle stabilization demands, as evidenced by the significant reduction in TA, LG, and MG muscle activations in Group~D, shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:meanEMG_solidground and summarized in Table~\ref{tbl:EMG_summarize .}}
elements.sent-2411-329: This, in turn, explains the observed decrease in TA muscle activity during the swing phase, highlighting how knee assistance indirectly benefits the entire lower limb and contributes to improved gait efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-330: The results demonstrate that the MPC consistently outperforms other conditions in reducing muscle activation and improving stability on sand, as evidenced by the notable reductions in muscle activations in Table~\ref{tbl:EMG_summarize , supporting the main claim that the MPC design offers more effective assistance than standard methods.}
elements.sent-2411-331: The observed redistribution of joint moments highlights a trade-off between reducing muscle activation and managing energy expenditure.
elements.sent-2411-332: By redistributing mechanical efforts among the lower limbs, the exoskeleton reduces localized muscle fatigue.
elements.sent-2411-333: Specifically, the MPC controller significantly reduced activation in several lower-limb muscles on both terrains as summarized in~Table~\ref{tbl:EMG_summarize , including ankle extensors and flexors (TA, LG, and MG).}
elements.sent-2411-334: In contrast, the unpowered exoskeleton (Group~B) increased muscle activation due to added weight and lack of active control, reinforcing the importance of adaptive torque assistance.
elements.sent-2411-335: These observations imply that refined exoskeleton design and control strategies can accommodate the complex walking dynamics on sand.
elements.sent-2411-336: Unpowered exoskeletons exacerbated high energy cost, increasing muscle activation and metabolic cost (see Figs.~\ref{fig:EMG_RMS and~\ref{fig:VO2Results ).}}
elements.sent-2411-337: It was reported that knee exoskeleton did not provide a benefit in the cases where small positive mechanical work was required at the knee joint such as walking on flat surfaces because of the additional mass of the exoskeleton itself~\cite{maclean2019energetics .}
elements.sent-2411-338: The unpowered mode and baseline controller increased metabolic cost by 9.4\% and 5.4\%, respectively, compared to unassisted walking, aligning with previous findings of 8-15\% increases on uneven terrain~\cite{kowalsky2021human .}
elements.sent-2411-339: Though, no statistical significance was found on the metabolic cost.
elements.sent-2411-340: One possible reason for this lack of significance could be differences in the physical fitness of subjects, with some participants potentially having strong endurance or conditioning, which may need further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-341: The above findings have significant implications for rehabilitation and assistive device design.
elements.sent-2411-342: The ability of the proposed MPC to enhance gait stability and reduce muscle activation on sand suggests potential applications for individuals with lower-limb impairments, particularly in environments with variable terrain compliance.
elements.sent-2411-343: By redistributing mechanical effort and stabilizing joint movements, the exoskeleton can improve user's mobility and reduce muscle fatigue.
elements.sent-2411-344: On the other hand, a discrepancy was observed between muscle activation reduction and metabolic cost.
elements.sent-2411-345: For instance, the unpowered exoskeleton (Group~B) significantly increased metabolic cost by 9.4\%.
elements.sent-2411-346: While the baseline controller (Group~C) reduced activation in the RF, VM, BF, and TA muscles on sand, the overall metabolic cost increased.
elements.sent-2411-347: These findings may be due to the extra effort required to carry the exoskeleton and stabilize other joints, which offset the benefits of knee assistance.
elements.sent-2411-348: Incorporating multi-joint coordination into the control framework could further enhance stability and reduce metabolic cost by distributing effort more effectively across the lower limbs.
elements.sent-2411-349: The experiment setup and design are further needed for accurate GRF measurements.
elements.sent-2411-350: We plan to explore the interplay between spatial and temporal features to develop adaptive control strategies for various walking conditions, including different terrains and speeds, ultimately enhancing the system's robustness and real-world applicability.
elements.sent-2411-351: The results also demonstrated that knee exoskeletons can help redistribute the kinematic and kinetic changes among the lower-limb joints.
